title: MariaDB passwords should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: MariaDB/MySQL connection URL with embedded password detected. This connection string contains authentication credentials in plaintext format (mysql://user:password@host or mariadb://user:password@host), providing direct database access. Exposing this allows unauthorized parties to execute queries, read sensitive data, modify records, and potentially compromise the entire database. Store credentials securely using environment variables or secrets management systems.

MariaDB/MySQL JDBC connection string with embedded password detected. JDBC URLs with password parameters (jdbc:mysql://host/db?password=xxx or jdbc:mariadb://host/db?password=xxx) expose database credentials in application code or configuration files. This grants full database access including read, write, and administrative operations. Remove passwords from connection strings and use secure credential management.

MariaDB/MySQL password detected in configuration. Database passwords in configuration files, environment variables, or application code provide authentication to database servers. Exposing these credentials allows unauthorized access to read, modify, or delete data, execute administrative commands, and potentially compromise connected applications. Use environment variables, secrets managers (like HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager), or encrypted configuration files instead.
